trajectory.description: Starting first iteration: need to build baseline GNN for ogbg-molpcba. Will follow recommended workflow: write model.py, train.py, predict.py, train with few epochs (3) and submit eval to measure iteration time and get baseline score. Model: GIN with edge features, hidden_dim=256, num_layers=3, global mean pooling, 128-task binary classification. Loss: BCEWithLogitsLoss masked by NaN. Evaluation: AP per task averaged across 128 tasks. Batch size: 256. Optimizer: Adam lr=1e-3. Data loading: custom dataset from .pt lists. Logging to metrics.jsonl.

Observation: Training crashed with segfault (exit code 139) after 8min, no output. Analysis: Likely GPU OOM or high memory pressure from hidden_dim=256, batch_size=256, 4 workers. Decision: Reduce hidden_dim to 128, batch_size to 128, num_workers=2, epochs=3 for safe baseline run. Also ensure proper error handling and flushing.

Observation: Previous two training attempts both segfaulted (exit 139) without producing output, even after reducing model size/batch size. Analysis: Crashes may stem from DataLoader multiprocessing (num_workers>0) or data loading memory pressure. Need to isolate the cause. Decision: Run a minimal diagnostic script that imports modules, loads a small subset of data, and does one forward pass with num_workers=0. This will confirm whether the environment is functional and guide next training attempt.

Observation: segfault (exit 139) persists across iterations with num_workers=2, indicating PyG multiprocessing incompatibility with CUDA environment. Analysis: All training attempts with DataLoader workers > 0 crash before producing output. Using num_workers=0 should avoid the issue entirely, trading speed for stability. Decision: Rewrite train.py with num_workers=0, add checkpoint saving (best val AP), run 3 epochs to measure baseline AP and determine per-epoch time. Update predict.py to num_workers=0 to ensure reliable evaluation.

Observation: Four training attempts have all segfaulted (exit 139), even with num_workers=0, suggesting a fundamental issue with PyG DataLoader or memory pressure when loading full datasets. Test_env succeeded with 128-graph subset, so code is functional on small data. Analysis: Full dataset loading may exceed RAM (48GB) when combined with DataLoader batching, or PyG's DataLoader has hidden multiprocessing (pin_memory threads). Decision: Eliminate DataLoader completely; use manual batching via PyG's Batch.from_data_list. Train on a small subset (5000 graphs) for 2 epochs to confirm pipeline works and obtain baseline AP. Also fix model to handle edge features properly in all GINE layers (project edge_attr to hidden_dim). Then submit eval to get first score and measure iteration time.

Observation: Iter 5 segfaulted quickly, even with manual batching and subset of 5000 graphs. Analysis: Crash likely due to memory pressure or a PyG bug triggered by large Data object lists. Decision: Further reduce subset to 1000 graphs, avoid storing all batches in memory by building Data objects per subset and batching on-the-fly. Train for 3 epochs, save checkpoint, then submit eval to get first baseline and measure iteration time. Also ensure predict.py works for eval.

Observation: Iter 6 segfaulted again even with manual batching and 1000-graph subset. Analysis: The crashes are not due to DataLoader workers (we used manual batching); the test_env.py succeeded with DataLoader on 128 graphs, so PyG DataLoader works on a small scale. Decision: Switch to PyG DataLoader with num_workers=0, pin_memory=False, and batch_size=32. Use 1000-graph subset for 3 epochs to establish a first baseline and measure iteration time. Also update predict.py to use DataLoader for robustness in eval. Submit eval after training to get baseline score and confirm pipeline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 7 segfaulted quickly (6.4s) with DataLoader on 1000 graphs. Test_env succeeded on 128 graphs, so crash is data-size related. Analysis: Possible cause is invalid edge indices (out-of-bounds) in some graphs, which would segfault in PyG's message passing. Small subset may avoid by chance. Decision: Run a diagnostic script to validate edge_index ranges for first 10000 graphs. If invalid graphs exist, filter them in training to prevent crashes. Then retrain on a small, clean subset to establish a baseline and measure iteration time.

Observation: data_check.py (167s) verified edge indices for 10k graphs — no invalid graphs. So crashes not due to data corruption. Past training attempts all segfaulted even with 1000 graphs and manual batching, yet test_env on 128 graphs succeeded. This suggests a memory corruption or cumulative GPU memory leak that appears after multiple batches. Decision: Write a thorough debug_loader.py that runs 500 graphs for 2 epochs, logs memory after each batch, and uses try/except to capture segfault location. If it succeeds, we'll know the pipeline works for larger sets; if it crashes, we'll see where. After debug, we will retrain on 1000–2000 graphs for a few epochs and submit eval to finally get a baseline metric and measure iteration time.

Observation: All previous training attempts segfault, even with 1000 graphs and manual batching. data_check confirms no invalid edge indices, so crash likely in GINEConv message passing or GPU memory corruption. Simple pooling without message passing (DeepSets baseline) may avoid the crash. Decision: Implement a SimpleMLP arch that encodes node features, pools globally (mean), and predicts 128 tasks; set as default for training. Run 1000-graph training for 3 epochs to verify pipeline works and get baseline AP. Then submit eval to measure iteration time and initial metric.

Observation: 10 iterations of segfault (exit 139) without any training completion; even SimpleMLP crashes. Data checks found no invalid edges. Analysis: Persistent segfault suggests memory exhaustion from loading full 350k graphs or GPU compatibility issue with DataLoader. Since test_env succeeded with 128 graphs, the likely culprit is peak RAM when loading full data. Decision: Create train_minimal.py that loads only 2000 graphs from file but deletes full data before training. Set device='cpu' to eliminate GPU crashes. Train SimpleMLP 2 epochs. If successful, submit eval for first baseline score and measure iteration time. If still crashes, narrow down further.

trajectory.description: Observation: 10 iterations of segfault (exit 139) across various models, batch sizes, and data subsets. Even SimpleMLP on CPU with 2000 graphs crashes. Only test_env with 128 graphs succeeds, suggesting a latent problem with PyG DataLoader or GPU memory that prevents reproducible training at scale. Analysis: The consistent segfaults indicate the current stack (PyG + GPU) is unreliable for this task. A safer baseline uses precomputed graph-level features (mean, std, histograms of node/edge attributes) and a standard MLP classifier, avoiding PyG entirely for training. This guarantees execution and yields a first metric. Decision: Write a preprocess script to extract fixed-size graph features from the full dataset (CPU, chunked), save to .npz, then train an MLP on these features. Update predict.py to compute features on-the-fly for test data. Submit eval after 10-epoch training to finally obtain a baseline AP and measure per-epoch time.

trajectory.description: Observation: All previous training attempts (including SimpleMLP on preprocessed features) crash with segfault or std::bad_alloc. Only test_env (128 graphs) succeeded, suggesting a memory corruption or leak after many batches. Analysis: The crash may originate from torch_geometric imports in model.py, which allocate CUDA extensions or cause memory fragmentation even when GNN layers are not used. By isolating to a pure PyTorch LinearMLP model (no torch_geometric) and forcing CPU, we eliminate potential sources of instability. Additionally, we use smaller hidden_dim=64, batch_size=32, and only 5 epochs to minimize memory pressure and get a baseline metric. Decision: Create model_linear.py, train_torch.py, and update predict.py to use the new model. Execute 5-epoch training on CPU. If successful, submit eval to finally obtain a baseline AP and measure per-epoch time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 13 finally completed training (no segfault) but AP crashed due to NaN in predictions, likely because SimpleMLP forward has wrong batch handling (collapses batch dim with mean) causing identical outputs and potential numerical issues, plus possible NaN in preprocessed data from prior bad alloc. Analysis: The core issue is that SimpleMLP is not a proper graph-level MLP; it processes batched features through node_embedding then pools incorrectly. Also preprocessed .npz may be corrupted. Decision: Write a chunked, memory-safe preprocessing script (preprocess_features_v2.py) that uses memmap to build X/Y without peak memory spikes, compute stats, and save clean .npz. Write a new FeatureMLP model (model_linear.py) that directly maps fixed-size features to 128 outputs. Update train.py to use FeatureMLP with pos_weight for class imbalance, proper batch training via DataLoader, and AP evaluation. Train 15 epochs on full dataset (350k graphs) – should be fast (~seconds/epoch). Then submit eval to get first baseline AP.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 14 training failed because train.py incorrectly flagged NaN in Y (labels) as error, though NaN labels are expected for missing annotations. Preprocessing succeeded and saved clean X. Analysis: Fix the NaN check to only check X for NaN; Y naturally contains NaN and will be masked in loss. After fix, training on full 350k graphs with FeatureMLP should complete and produce a checkpoint. Decision: Update train.py to correct NaN check, add gradient clipping, normalize features inside training (mean/std from preprocessing), train for 20 epochs with hidden_dim=512, batch_size=512 on GPU, then submit eval to finally get a baseline AP and measure per-epoch time.

trajectory.description: Observation: iter 15 training crashed during validation AP computation because model predictions contained NaN (likely due to logit explosion from pos_weight imbalance). No metric obtained. Analysis: The FeatureMLP training is numerically unstable; we need to clamp logits, handle NaN predictions in AP evaluation, and cap pos_weight more strictly (max 10). Decision: Update train.py with robust NaN handling (clamp logits at ±10, replace NaN predictions with 0.0, mask NaN predictions from AP), plus clip pos_weight to 10. Update predict.py similarly to clamp logits and replace NaN outputs for reliability. Retrain with 10 epochs using same preprocessed v2 data and submit eval to finally get a baseline AP metric.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 16 training completed (no crash), but loss is NaN and val AP only 0.024. Eval submission crashed because predict.py used weights_only=True on checkpoint containing numpy arrays. Analysis: Low AP indicates graph-level aggregated features are insufficient; NaN loss suggests numerical issue (likely double-standardization causing extreme values). Evaluation obstruction requires predict.py fix to load with weights_only=False. Decision: Fix predict.py (weights_only=False), correct train.py (remove redundant normalization, add logit clamping and debugging), retrain FeatureMLP for 20 epochs to benchmark. If AP still <0.05, pivot to GNN on CPU subset to capture graph structure. Submit eval immediately after training to get first valid AP score and measure iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 17 training completed with val_AP 0.0242, loss NaN, test AP 0.0254. Analysis: NaN loss arises because BCEWithLogitsLoss produces NaN for NaN labels, and multiplying by mask (0 * NaN) yields NaN. This poisons gradients, preventing learning. Fix: zero out unlabeled entries before summing. Also, low AP may require richer features (RDKit fingerprints). Decision: Fix train.py NaN masking, retrain 30 epochs to see if AP improves. If AP remains low, then enhance with fingerprint features.

Observation: Baseline AP 0.025 with feature MLP, loss NaN, AP static across epochs (no learning). Aggregated features lose graph structure. Analysis: GNN with message passing necessary for competitive performance. Previous segfaults likely due to DataLoader multiprocessing/memory issues; pipeline stable now. Remaining 4.87h and 12 iterations allow GNN training on subset. Decision: Implement GINE model (edge-aware GIN), train on 50k subset for 10 epochs to verify stability and improve AP. Use num_workers=0, batch_size=64. Update predict.py accordingly. Submit eval after training.

Observation: Persistent segfaults (exit 139) across many GNN attempts, even with small subsets and manual batching. Analysis: The crashes are likely caused by PyG's custom CUDA/C++ extensions (e.g., torch-scatter) or DataLoader internals. To bypass these entirely, we implement a GIN from scratch using torch.scatter_add for message passing, no PyG imports. We'll train on CPU only with a 20k-graph subset, small batch size, and custom batching to ensure stability. Decision: Write model_gnn.py with pure PyTorch GIN layers, a new train script that collates graphs manually and uses masked BCE loss, and update predict.py to use the new model. Train 10 epochs on CPU, then submit eval to get a first stable GNN-based AP and measure iteration time. If AP improves over 0.025, we can scale up.

Observation: GNN training with pure PyTorch GIN (iter 20) produced NaN loss across all 10 epochs on CPU. Analysis: The custom GIN implementation likely lacked normalization (BatchNorm) and node feature scaling, causing numerical instability. Also, edge features were unused, which may lose important information. Decision: Overwrite model_gnn.py with a corrected implementation that includes BatchNorm after each GIN layer, residual connections, proper scatter_add and global mean pooling using pure torch operations, and node feature standardization computed from training subset. Write train_gnn_v2.py to train on 50k graphs with masked BCE loss, save checkpoint with mean/std, and submit eval after 20 epochs. This should give a stable non-NaN baseline and hopefully improve AP above 0.025.

trajectory.error_summary: bash: line 1:  2577 Segmentation fault      (core dumped) python train_gnn_v2.py --epochs 10 --n_train 20000 --hidden_dim 64 --num_layers 2 --lr 3e-4 --batch_size 64
trajectory.description: Observation: iter 21 GNN training completed without segfault but loss NaN from step 2, val AP NaN. The pure PyTorch GINEModel suffers numerical instability, possibly from exploding gradients or improper mask handling. Analysis: The first epoch had loss 6.98 but then NaN; likely a bad batch caused NaN loss, and the training loop did not skip that batch, corrupting parameters. Subsequent epochs all zero loss. To fix, we need to skip NaN batches, lower learning rate, add skip connection scaling, and use stable pooling functions. Decision: Update model_gnn.py with torch_scatter for pool, residual scaling (0.1), weight initialization (kaiming), and lower hidden_dim=64. Update train_gnn_v2.py to skip NaN batches, use lr=3e-4, train on 20k graphs for 10 epochs. Submit eval if val AP > -0.5. This is a short, safe test to confirm stability within ~20min. If successful, we can scale up next iteration; if still NaN, we'll pivot to WL-based graph fingerprint features + MLP.

Analysis: RDKit's Bond cannot be directly instantiated; must use Chem.RWMol() to build molecules. Fix mol_utils.py to construct molecules via RWMol, correctly adding atoms (atomic number from node features) and bonds (mapping bond_type from edge_attr to RDKit bond types). Then train MLP on Morgan fingerprints (2048 bits) to get stable baseline above 0.025. Decision: Rewrite mol_utils.py with correct atom/bond construction, write train_fp.py for fingerprint computation and training on full dataset, train 30 epochs, save best checkpoint, and submit eval immediately. trajectory.description: Observation: iter 24 train_fp.py crashed due to RDKit Pre-condition violation (RingInfo not initialized) when computing Morgan fingerprints. The make_mol function omitted ring perception after building the molecule. Analysis: Morgan fingerprints need ring info; we can force ring perception by calling Chem.GetSymmSSSR(mol) after sanitization (or instead of sanitization if it fails). Additionally, we should rewrite train_fp.py fully (current workspace truncation) to ensure a clean training script, using a smaller hidden_dim=256 and fewer epochs=20 for faster iteration, but still train on full fingerprint features to get a strong baseline. Fingerprint features should yield AP >> 0.025. We must submit eval as soon as training completes. Decision: Update mol_utils.py with proper ring initialization, write complete train_fp.py, run training for 20 epochs on GPU, then submit eval.

Observation: iter 25 train_fp.py crashed due to RDKit implicit hydrogen error after molecule construction. The root cause: we didn't set explicit atom properties (formal charge, num Hs) and didn't call UpdatePropertyCache, so RDKit's internal valence calculation failed. Analysis: With proper atom properties and UpdatePropertyCache, the molecule will be sanitized correctly, enabling Morgan fingerprint computation. Fingerprints should significantly boost AP over the 0.025 linear baseline. Decision: Fix mol_utils.py to set atom properties and update property cache, ensuring robust molecule building. Write complete train_fp.py to precompute fingerprints, train a FeatureMLP on 2048-bit fingerprints for 25 epochs, and save best checkpoint. This will finally provide a strong, stable pipeline. Run training now (expected ~1.5-2h for fingerprint precomputation + training). Submit eval in next iteration as separate step.

trajectory.description: Observation: iter 29 training crashed due to NaN in validation AP computation, despite fingerprint features. The previous training (iter 26) had loss=0.0 for all epochs, indicating that the masked BCE loss was incorrectly implemented (NaN propagation through mask). Analysis: The core problem is that binary_cross_entropy_with_logits computes NaN for entries where target is NaN, and multiplying by mask (0 * NaN) yields NaN. The fix: replace NaN targets with 0 before loss, then mask. Also, predictions must be clamed to avoid NaN. With correct loss, the fingerprint MLP should learn and improve AP beyond the baseline 0.025. Remaining time: 2.32h, only 1 iteration left. We must train and submit eval in the same action. Decision: Overwrite train_fp.py with a robust training loop (NaN-filled target, proper mask, gradient clipping, capped pos_weight, NaN-free AP). Train 50 epochs on precomputed fingerprints, then submit eval immediately. This should produce the first meaningful AP and use the last iteration effectively.
